news_summary: Recent breakthroughs in medical research show promising advances in epilepsy treatment, heart recovery, malaria prevention, and long-lasting relief for OCD symptoms.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées dans la recherche médicale montrent des progrès prometteurs dans le traitement de l'épilepsie, la récupération cardiaque, la prévention du paludisme et le soulagement durable des symptômes du TOC.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances en investigación médica muestran prometedores progresos en el tratamiento de la epilepsia, la recuperación cardíaca, la prevención de la malaria y el alivio duradero de los síntomas del TOC.

articles.title: New drug cuts seizures by up to 91% in children with rare epilepsy
articles.summary: A new experimental drug is showing remarkable promise for children with Dravet syndrome, a severe genetic form of epilepsy. In clinical trials, the treatment zorevunersen cut seizures by as much as 91% while also improving quality of life for many patients. The therapy works by boosting the function of a key gene involved in nerve cell signaling. Encouraging results have led researchers to launch a larger Phase 3 trial.
articles.content:
An experimental therapy for children with a severe and difficult to treat form of epilepsy appears to be both safe and highly effective at reducing seizures, according to results from an international clinical trial led by UCL (University College London) and Great Ormond Street Hospital. The findings suggest the treatment could significantly improve the health and daily lives of affected children.

The study, published in The New England Journal of Medicine, found that children with Dravet syndrome experienced seizure reductions of up to 91 percent while regularly receiving an investigational drug called zorevunersen.

Researchers also reported early evidence that the therapy may help ease some of the disorder's effects on thinking and behavior. Over a three year period, children participating in the study showed improvements in quality of life, and most reported side effects were mild.

Understanding Dravet Syndrome

Dravet syndrome is a rare and severe genetic epilepsy that causes frequent seizures that are often difficult to control. The condition is also linked to long term neurodevelopmental challenges, feeding problems, movement difficulties, and a higher risk of premature death.

For many families, treatment options remain limited. Existing medications fail to fully control seizures in many patients, and no currently approved therapies directly address the cognitive and behavioral complications associated with the disorder.

How the Drug Targets the Underlying Genetic Cause

Zorevunersen (produced by Stoke Therapeutics in collaboration with Biogen) is designed to address the root cause of Dravet syndrome by acting on a faulty gene.

Most people carry two copies of the SCN1A gene. In individuals with Dravet syndrome, one copy does not produce enough of a protein needed for proper nerve cell signaling.

The drug works by increasing production of this protein from the healthy copy of the SCN1A gene. By boosting protein levels, the therapy aims to restore more normal function in nerve cells.

Clinical Trial Results and Ongoing Research

The latest findings come from the initial trial and follow up extension studies, which together involved 81 children with Dravet syndrome in the United Kingdom and the United States.

These early studies were primarily designed to assess the safety and tolerability of zorevunersen. Researchers also monitored how the treatment affected seizure frequency, cognitive function, behavior, and overall quality of life. A larger Phase Three trial is currently underway to further evaluate the drug.

Lead author Professor Helen Cross, Director and Professor of Childhood Epilepsy at the UCL Institute of Child Health and an Honorary Consultant in Paediatric Neurology at Great Ormond Street Hospital (GOSH), said: "I regularly see patients with hard-to-treat genetic epilepsies with impacts that go beyond seizures and it's heart-breaking when treatment options are limited. This new treatment could help children with Dravet syndrome lead much healthier and happier lives.

"Overall, our findings showed that zorevunersen is safe to use and well tolerated by most patients and supports further evaluation in the ongoing Phase Three study."

Details of the Trial

A total of 81 children between the ages of two and 18 participated in the initial clinical trial. Before starting treatment, these patients experienced an average of 17 seizures each month.

Participants received doses of up to 70mg of zorevunersen through a lumbar puncture. Some children received a single dose, while others were given additional doses two or three months later during a six month treatment period.

Seventy five of the children later continued into extension studies, where they received the medication every four months.

Among those who received the 70mg dose during the first stage of the trial, seizure frequency dropped between 59 percent and 91 percent during the first 20 months of the extension studies compared with the number of seizures recorded before treatment began.

Hospitals Involved in the Study

Nineteen participants were treated at hospitals in the United Kingdom. In addition to Great Ormond Street Hospital, participating centers included Sheffield Children's Hospital, Evelina London Children's Hospital, and The Royal Hospital for Children in Glasgow.

At GOSH, the study took place at the National Institute of Health and Care Research's Clinical Research Facility, a specialized center dedicated to running experimental clinical trials involving children.

Dravet Syndrome UK Chair of Trustees Galia Wilson said: "We regularly see the devastating impact that this condition has on the lives of families. That's why we're so thrilled about these latest results from the initial zorevunersen clinical trials.

"We're now looking forward to the Phase Three clinical trials taking place to see if the early promise we see here will translate into real hope for all those families currently affected by Dravet Syndrome."

Patient Story

Freddie, an eight year old patient from Huddersfield who receives care through Sheffield Children's NHS Foundation Trust, participated in the trial.

After starting the treatment in 2021, Freddie's seizure pattern changed dramatically. He went from experiencing more than a dozen seizures during the night to having just one or two brief seizures lasting only seconds every three to five days.

His mother Lauren said: "The trial has completely changed our lives. We now have a life we didn't ever think was possible and most importantly it's a life that Freddie can enjoy."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough with a new drug that dramatically reduces seizures in children with a severe genetic epilepsy, improving their quality of life. The impact is broad and meaningful for affected families and the medical community, supported by detailed clinical trial data and ongoing research.
articles.personality_title: New drug cuts seizures by up to 91% in children with rare epilepsy
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Dravet syndrome is a rare and severe form of epilepsy that starts in childhood. It causes frequent seizures that are hard to control and can also affect thinking, behavior, and movement. Most current treatments do not fully stop seizures or help with other problems caused by the disease.

**What happened** – Researchers tested a new drug called zorevunersen on 81 children with Dravet syndrome in the UK and the US. The drug works by fixing a gene problem that causes the disease. In the trial, some children had their seizures reduced by as much as 91%. The treatment was given through lumbar puncture (a method to deliver medicine near the spine) and was found to be safe with mostly mild side effects. The study also showed improvements in the children’s quality of life.

**Impact** – This drug is important because it does more than just reduce seizures. It targets the root genetic cause of Dravet syndrome, which no current treatments do. For many families, this means a big change in daily life, as seizures become less frequent and some thinking and behavior problems may improve. For example, an 8-year-old boy named Freddie went from having many seizures every night to just one or two short ones every few days.

**What's next step** – A larger Phase Three trial is now underway to confirm these positive results and test the drug on more patients. If successful, this could lead to zorevunersen becoming an approved treatment for Dravet syndrome, giving hope to many families.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new gene-targeting drug has shown it can safely reduce seizures by up to 91% in children with Dravet syndrome, offering a promising new treatment option.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau médicament réduit les crises de 91 % chez les enfants atteints d’une épilepsie rare
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le syndrome de Dravet est une forme rare et grave d’épilepsie qui débute dans l’enfance. Il provoque des crises fréquentes difficiles à contrôler et affecte aussi la pensée, le comportement et les mouvements. La plupart des traitements actuels ne stoppent pas complètement les crises ni les autres problèmes liés à cette maladie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont testé un nouveau médicament appelé zorevunersen sur 81 enfants atteints du syndrome de Dravet au Royaume-Uni et aux États-Unis. Le médicament agit en corrigeant un problème génétique qui cause la maladie. Lors de l’essai, certains enfants ont vu leurs crises diminuer jusqu’à 91 %. Le traitement, administré par ponction lombaire (injection près de la colonne vertébrale), s’est révélé sûr avec surtout des effets secondaires légers. L’étude a aussi montré une amélioration de la qualité de vie des enfants.

**Impact** – Ce médicament est important car il agit sur la cause génétique du syndrome de Dravet, ce que les traitements actuels ne font pas. Pour de nombreuses familles, cela change beaucoup la vie quotidienne, avec moins de crises et une amélioration possible des troubles cognitifs et comportementaux. Par exemple, un garçon de 8 ans nommé Freddie est passé de nombreuses crises chaque nuit à seulement une ou deux courtes crises tous les quelques jours.

**Prochaine étape** – Un essai clinique de Phase Trois plus large est en cours pour confirmer ces résultats positifs et tester le médicament sur plus de patients. Si cela réussit, zorevunersen pourrait devenir un traitement approuvé pour le syndrome de Dravet, offrant de l’espoir à beaucoup de familles.

**Phrase clé** – Un nouveau médicament ciblant un gène a montré qu’il peut réduire en toute sécurité les crises jusqu’à 91 % chez les enfants atteints du syndrome de Dravet, ouvrant une nouvelle voie de traitement prometteuse.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo medicamento reduce las convulsiones hasta en un 91 % en niños con epilepsia rara
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El síndrome de Dravet es una forma rara y grave de epilepsia que comienza en la infancia. Causa convulsiones frecuentes que son difíciles de controlar y también afecta el pensamiento, el comportamiento y el movimiento. La mayoría de los tratamientos actuales no detienen completamente las convulsiones ni ayudan con otros problemas de la enfermedad.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores probaron un nuevo medicamento llamado zorevunersen en 81 niños con síndrome de Dravet en Reino Unido y Estados Unidos. El medicamento funciona arreglando un problema genético que causa la enfermedad. En el ensayo, algunos niños redujeron sus convulsiones hasta un 91 %. El tratamiento se administró mediante una punción lumbar (una forma de dar medicina cerca de la columna vertebral) y resultó seguro con efectos secundarios leves. El estudio también mostró mejoras en la calidad de vida de los niños.

**Impacto** – Este medicamento es importante porque actúa sobre la causa genética del síndrome de Dravet, cosa que los tratamientos actuales no hacen. Para muchas familias, esto significa un gran cambio en la vida diaria, con menos convulsiones y posible mejora en problemas de pensamiento y comportamiento. Por ejemplo, un niño de 8 años llamado Freddie pasó de tener muchas convulsiones cada noche a solo una o dos cortas cada pocos días.

**Próximo paso** – Ahora se está realizando un ensayo clínico más grande de Fase Tres para confirmar estos resultados positivos y probar el medicamento en más pacientes. Si tiene éxito, zorevunersen podría convertirse en un tratamiento aprobado para el síndrome de Dravet, ofreciendo esperanza a muchas familias.

**Frase clave** – Un nuevo medicamento que actúa sobre un gen ha demostrado que puede reducir de forma segura las convulsiones hasta en un 91 % en niños con síndrome de Dravet, ofreciendo una opción de tratamiento prometedora.
articles.image_prompt: A gentle, detailed painting of a small glowing neuron gently wrapped and nurtured by protective hands made of soft light, surrounded by a calm, abstract representation of a child’s brain with subtle, warm hues symbolizing healing and hope, conveying the breakthrough of a new therapy restoring nerve function and dramatically reducing seizures in children with rare epilepsy.

articles.title: Ozempic-like weight loss drugs may help the heart recover after a heart attack
articles.summary: Popular weight-loss drugs such as Ozempic, Wegovy, and Mounjaro may do more than help people shed pounds. New research suggests these GLP-1 medications could also help protect the heart after a heart attack by restoring blood flow in tiny blood vessels that often remain blocked even after doctors reopen a major artery.
articles.content:
Medications designed to promote weight loss by mimicking GLP-1 may also help limit additional heart damage after a heart attack. A new study led by researchers at the University of Bristol and University College London (UCL) found that these drugs could reduce the risk of serious complications that occur in up to half of heart attack patients.

The findings, published in Nature Communications, suggest that GLP-1 weight-loss drugs might offer a new strategy to improve recovery after a heart attack.

Earlier research has already shown that GLP-1 weight-loss medications can lower the likelihood of major heart problems. Notably, these benefits appear regardless of a person's existing health conditions or how much weight they lose while taking the drugs.

Scientists Investigate How GLP-1 Drugs Protect the Heart

To better understand why these medications benefit the heart, researchers examined the biological processes involved. Their earlier work had shown that small contractile cells called pericytes tighten coronary capillaries during the early stages of ischemia, a condition that occurs when the heart muscle is deprived of oxygen-rich blood.

In the new study, the team explored whether GLP-1 drugs could counteract this process and reopen the tiny blood vessels that become blocked.

Dr. Svetlana Mastitskaya, Senior Lecturer in Cardiovascular Regenerative Medicine at Bristol Medical School: Translational Health Sciences (THS) and the study's lead author, explained: "In nearly half of all heart attack patients, tiny blood vessels within the heart muscle remain narrowed, even after the main artery is cleared during emergency medical treatment. This results in a complication known as 'no-reflow,' where blood is unable to reach certain parts of the heart tissue.

"Our previous research has shown that this narrowing of blood vessels contributes significantly to 'no-reflow,' a complication that increases the risk of death or hospital admission for heart failure within a year of a heart attack. But our latest findings are surprising in that we have found GLP-1 drugs may prevent this problem."

GLP-1 Drugs Improve Blood Flow in the Heart

Experiments using animal models revealed that GLP-1 drugs improve blood flow in the heart after a heart attack. The medications activate potassium channels, which relax pericytes and allow previously constricted blood vessels to widen. As a result, blood can reach heart tissue more effectively, lowering the chance of additional damage.

Professor David Attwell, Jodrell Professor of Physiology at UCL, and the study's co-lead, added: "With an increasing number of similar GLP-1 drugs now being used in clinical practice, for conditions ranging from type 2 diabetes and obesity to kidney disease, our findings highlight the potential for these existing drugs to be repurposed to treat the risk of 'no-reflow' in heart attack patients, offering a potentially life-saving solution."

Dr. Svetlana Mastitskaya is funded by the British Heart Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough showing that popular weight-loss drugs (GLP-1 medications like Ozempic) can also protect the heart after a heart attack by improving blood flow in tiny vessels, potentially reducing serious complications and mortality. This discovery has broad implications for heart attack recovery and could benefit a large population, supported by detailed scientific research.
articles.personality_title: Weight-loss drugs may help the heart heal after a heart attack
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Heart attacks happen when blood flow to the heart muscle is blocked. Even after doctors open the main blocked artery, tiny blood vessels in the heart can stay narrow, stopping blood from reaching parts of the heart. This problem is called "no-reflow" and can cause more damage or lead to heart failure.

**What happened** – Researchers from the University of Bristol and University College London studied popular weight-loss drugs like Ozempic that mimic a natural hormone called GLP-1. They found these drugs can relax tiny cells called pericytes that tighten small blood vessels, helping to reopen these vessels after a heart attack. This allows blood to flow better to the heart muscle.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because up to half of heart attack patients suffer from the no-reflow problem, increasing their risk of serious complications or death. Using GLP-1 drugs could reduce this risk by improving blood flow inside the heart, offering a new way to protect the heart after a heart attack beyond just opening the main artery.

**What's next step** – The researchers suggest that existing GLP-1 weight-loss drugs might be tested and used to treat heart attack patients to prevent no-reflow. Future studies and clinical trials will likely explore how best to use these drugs for heart recovery.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Weight-loss drugs that act on GLP-1 show promise in helping tiny heart vessels reopen after a heart attack, improving recovery and reducing complications.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les médicaments contre la perte de poids peuvent aider le cœur à guérir après une crise cardiaque
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les crises cardiaques surviennent lorsque le flux sanguin vers le muscle cardiaque est bloqué. Même après que les médecins ont débloqué l'artère principale, de petits vaisseaux sanguins dans le cœur peuvent rester étroits, empêchant le sang d’atteindre certaines zones du cœur. Ce problème s'appelle le « no-reflow » et peut causer plus de dégâts ou conduire à une insuffisance cardiaque.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs des universités de Bristol et de Londres ont étudié des médicaments populaires pour la perte de poids comme Ozempic qui imitent une hormone naturelle appelée GLP-1. Ils ont découvert que ces médicaments peuvent détendre de petites cellules appelées péricytes qui resserrent les petits vaisseaux sanguins, aidant à rouvrir ces vaisseaux après une crise cardiaque. Cela permet un meilleur flux sanguin vers le muscle cardiaque.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car jusqu’à la moitié des patients après une crise cardiaque souffrent du problème de no-reflow, ce qui augmente leur risque de complications graves ou de décès. Utiliser les médicaments GLP-1 pourrait réduire ce risque en améliorant le flux sanguin à l’intérieur du cœur, offrant une nouvelle façon de protéger le cœur après une crise.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs suggèrent que ces médicaments déjà utilisés pour la perte de poids soient testés et employés pour traiter les patients après une crise cardiaque afin de prévenir le no-reflow. Des études et essais cliniques futurs examineront comment mieux utiliser ces médicaments pour la récupération cardiaque.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les médicaments contre la perte de poids agissant sur le GLP-1 pourraient aider à rouvrir les petits vaisseaux du cœur après une crise cardiaque, améliorant la récupération et réduisant les complications.
articles.personality_title_es: Medicamentos para bajar de peso pueden ayudar al corazón a recuperarse tras un infarto
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los infartos ocurren cuando el flujo de sangre al músculo del corazón se bloquea. Aunque los doctores abran la arteria principal bloqueada, pequeños vasos sanguíneos dentro del corazón pueden seguir estrechos, impidiendo que la sangre llegue a ciertas partes del músculo. Este problema se llama "no-reflujo" y puede causar más daño o insuficiencia cardíaca.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Bristol y University College London estudiaron medicamentos populares para bajar de peso como Ozempic, que imitan una hormona llamada GLP-1. Descubrieron que estos medicamentos pueden relajar células pequeñas llamadas pericitos que estrechan los vasos sanguíneos, ayudando a abrir estos vasos después de un infarto. Esto mejora el flujo de sangre al músculo cardíaco.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque hasta la mitad de los pacientes con infarto sufren el problema de no-reflujo, aumentando el riesgo de complicaciones graves o muerte. Usar medicamentos GLP-1 podría reducir este riesgo al mejorar el flujo sanguíneo dentro del corazón, ofreciendo una nueva forma de proteger el corazón después del infarto.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores sugieren que estos medicamentos ya usados para bajar de peso se prueben y usen para tratar a pacientes con infarto y prevenir el no-reflujo. Se esperan estudios y ensayos clínicos para saber cómo usarlos mejor en la recuperación cardíaca.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los medicamentos para bajar de peso que actúan sobre GLP-1 podrían ayudar a reabrir pequeños vasos del corazón tras un infarto, mejorando la recuperación y reduciendo complicaciones.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vibrant heart-shaped network of tiny glowing capillaries gently expanding and flowing with bright red blood, surrounded by delicate, relaxed pericyte-like cells depicted as soft, flexible rings loosening their grip, set against a calm, neutral background with natural, muted colors emphasizing healing and renewed circulation.

articles.title: Scientists discover the protein that malaria parasites can’t live without
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered a crucial weakness in the malaria parasite that could open the door to new treatments. Researchers identified a protein called Aurora-related kinase 1 (ARK1) that acts like a traffic controller during the parasite’s unusual cell division process, ensuring its genetic material is properly separated as it multiplies. When scientists switched off ARK1 in laboratory experiments, the parasite could no longer replicate correctly and failed to complete its life cycle in both humans and mosquitoes—effectively halting its ability to spread.
articles.content:
Researchers from several international institutions have uncovered new details about how the malaria parasite grows and spreads. Their work has identified a specialized protein that the parasite needs in order to survive and move between hosts, making it a promising target for future antimalarial drugs.

The discovery focuses on a molecule known as Aurora-related kinase 1 (ARK1). In a study published in Nature Communications, scientists from the University of Nottingham, the National Institute of Immunology (NII) in India, the University of Groningen in the Netherlands, the Francis Crick Institute, and other collaborators found that ARK1 functions like a cellular traffic controller during the parasite's unusual process of growth and division.

Understanding Malaria Parasite Growth

Malaria continues to rank among the deadliest infectious diseases worldwide. It is caused by Plasmodium parasites, which rapidly multiply inside both human hosts and mosquitoes. Learning how these parasites divide and reproduce is critical for finding ways to stop the disease.

The malaria parasite divides very differently from human cells. Instead of following the typical pattern seen in human biology, it uses a more unusual and complex method of growth. The researchers discovered that ARK1 plays a central role in organizing the spindle, the cellular structure that separates genetic material so new parasite cells can form.

Disabling ARK1 Stops Parasite Development

When scientists disabled ARK1 in laboratory experiments, parasite development quickly broke down. Without the protein, the parasites failed to build proper spindles, which prevented them from dividing correctly.

As a result, the parasites could not continue their life cycle. They were unable to fully develop inside either the human host or the mosquito, effectively blocking the chain of transmission that allows malaria to spread.

"The name 'Aurora' refers to the Roman goddess of dawn, and we believe this protein truly heralds a new beginning in our understanding of malaria cell biology," said Dr. Ryuji Yanase first author of the study from the School of Life Sciences at the University of Nottingham.

A Potential Target for New Malaria Drugs

Because the malaria parasite moves through different stages in both humans and mosquitoes, understanding its biology requires collaboration across many research groups.

"Plasmodium divides via distinct processes in the human and mosquito host, it was well and truly a team effort, which allowed us to appreciate the role of ARK1 almost simultaneously in the two hosts and shed light on novel aspects of parasite biology," said Annu Nagar and Dr. Pushkar Sharma from the Biotechnology Research and Innovation Council (BRIC)-NII, New Delhi.

Researchers are particularly encouraged by how different the parasite's ARK1 system is from the equivalent proteins found in human cells.

"What makes this discovery so exciting is that the malaria parasite's 'Aurora' complex is very different from the version found in human cells. This divergence is a huge advantage," Professor Tewari added. "It means we can potentially design drugs that target the parasite's ARK1 specifically, turning the lights out on malaria without harming the patient."

By revealing how this unusual molecular machinery operates, the research provides a clearer roadmap for developing drugs that disrupt the parasite's life cycle and ultimately prevent malaria transmission.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery identifying a protein essential for malaria parasite survival, which offers a promising target for new antimalarial drugs. This breakthrough has broad potential impact on global health by advancing efforts to combat one of the deadliest infectious diseases worldwide. The detailed explanation of the protein's role and the collaborative international research provide substantial context and substance.
articles.personality_title: Scientists identify key protein that malaria parasites need to survive
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Malaria is a dangerous disease caused by tiny parasites called Plasmodium. These parasites grow and multiply inside both humans and mosquitoes, spreading the disease. Finding out how the parasite grows can help scientists develop new treatments.

**What happened** – Researchers from several countries discovered a protein called Aurora-related kinase 1 (ARK1) that acts like a traffic controller during the parasite’s unusual cell division. When scientists turned off ARK1 in the lab, the parasites could not divide properly and failed to complete their life cycle in humans and mosquitoes.

**Impact** – This protein is very different from similar proteins in human cells, which means drugs can be designed to target the parasite without harming people. By stopping the parasite from multiplying, these drugs could block the spread of malaria in a new way.

**What's next step** – Scientists will use this discovery to develop medicines that block ARK1. Future research will test these drugs to see if they can safely stop malaria parasites in humans and mosquitoes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A newly found protein essential for malaria parasites offers a promising target to stop the disease from spreading without harming human cells.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques identifient une protéine clé dont les parasites du paludisme ont besoin pour survivre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le paludisme est une maladie dangereuse causée par de petits parasites appelés Plasmodium. Ces parasites grandissent et se multiplient à l’intérieur des humains et des moustiques, ce qui propage la maladie. Comprendre comment le parasite se développe peut aider les scientifiques à créer de nouveaux traitements.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de plusieurs pays ont découvert une protéine appelée kinase liée à Aurora 1 (ARK1) qui agit comme un contrôleur de trafic pendant la division cellulaire inhabituelle du parasite. Quand les scientifiques ont désactivé ARK1 en laboratoire, les parasites ne pouvaient plus se diviser correctement et ne terminaient pas leur cycle de vie chez l’humain et le moustique.

**Impact** – Cette protéine est très différente des protéines similaires dans les cellules humaines, ce qui signifie que des médicaments peuvent cibler le parasite sans nuire aux personnes. En arrêtant la multiplication du parasite, ces médicaments pourraient bloquer la propagation du paludisme d’une nouvelle façon.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques utiliseront cette découverte pour développer des médicaments qui bloquent ARK1. Les recherches futures testeront ces médicaments afin de vérifier s’ils peuvent arrêter en toute sécurité les parasites du paludisme chez les humains et les moustiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une protéine nouvellement découverte, essentielle aux parasites du paludisme, offre une cible prometteuse pour stopper la maladie sans endommager les cellules humaines.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos identifican proteína clave que los parásitos de la malaria necesitan para sobrevivir
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La malaria es una enfermedad peligrosa causada por pequeños parásitos llamados Plasmodium. Estos parásitos crecen y se multiplican dentro de humanos y mosquitos, propagando la enfermedad. Entender cómo crecen puede ayudar a los científicos a crear nuevos tratamientos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de varios países descubrieron una proteína llamada quinasa relacionada con Aurora 1 (ARK1) que actúa como un controlador de tráfico durante la inusual división celular del parásito. Cuando apagaron ARK1 en el laboratorio, los parásitos no pudieron dividirse correctamente y no completaron su ciclo de vida en humanos y mosquitos.

**Impacto** – Esta proteína es muy diferente a las similares en las células humanas, lo que significa que se pueden diseñar medicamentos que ataquen al parásito sin dañar a las personas. Al detener la multiplicación del parásito, estos medicamentos podrían bloquear la propagación de la malaria de una manera nueva.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán este descubrimiento para desarrollar medicamentos que bloqueen ARK1. Investigaciones futuras probarán estos medicamentos para ver si pueden detener de forma segura a los parásitos en humanos y mosquitos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una proteína recién descubierta esencial para los parásitos de la malaria ofrece una meta prometedora para detener la enfermedad sin dañar las células humanas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, intricately woven cellular spindle structure shaped like delicate threads of light, held gently by abstract, stylized molecular shapes symbolizing the Aurora-related kinase 1 protein, set against a soft, natural background that subtly merges human and mosquito silhouettes in the distance, representing the malaria parasite’s life cycle and the hopeful breakthrough in stopping its spread.

articles.title: Just one dose of psilocybin relieves symptoms of OCD for months
articles.summary: Taking psilocybin – the psychedelic component of magic mushrooms – eased symptoms of obsessive compulsive disorder among people who did not respond to conventional treatments, and the effects lasted at least several months
articles.content:
Taking psilocybin – the psychedelic component of magic mushrooms – eased symptoms of obsessive compulsive disorder among people who did not respond to conventional treatments, and the effects lasted at least several months

Magic mushrooms are one of the psychedelics showing real medical potential John Moore/Getty Images

A single dose of psilocybin – the active ingredient in magic mushrooms – produces rapid reductions in symptoms of obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), according to the first placebo-controlled trial to test the psychedelic for the mental health condition. The effects also seem to persist for at least 12 weeks, suggesting that psilocybin could provide lasting relief.

“If we give you a trip, we think we can break the cycles of obsessive thinking and behaviour,” says David Nutt at Imperial College London, who wasn’t involved in the research. “The whole point of OCD therapy is about teaching people to behave differently. So, rather than check the lights 15 times, you check them twice.”

About 1 to 3 per cent of people have OCD, a condition characterised by obsessive thoughts and compulsive habits, which can be overwhelming. Treatments tend to include talking therapies and antidepressants, but between 40 and 60 per cent of people with OCD don’t respond to them.

Psilocybin – and other psychedelics like ketamine – have shown promise for mental health conditions. To get a more robust idea of its potential, Christopher Pittenger at Yale School of Medicine and his colleagues decided to test psilocybin for OCD in the first randomised, placebo-controlled trial.

The team recruited 28 adults who had lived with OCD for two decades, on average, and had unsuccessfully tried at least two different treatments. They all had the severity of their symptoms rated using a standard scale, which gives a score of between 0 and 40. They were then randomly assigned to either receive a single oral dose of psilocybin (0.25 milligrams per kilogram of body weight) or niacin (250 milligrams), also known as vitamin B3, which acted as the placebo.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

The psilocybin dose was enough to give the participants a psychedelic trip, which tends to involve big changes in perception, thoughts and emotion. “It’s pretty intense, though it varies from person to person,” says Pittenger.

After 48 hours, the symptom scores of the 14 people who received psilocybin decreased by 9.76 points, on average, while those taking niacin stayed about the same. “The speed and durability of the improvement seen after a single dose of psilocybin are remarkable,” says Alex Kwan at Cornell University in Ithaca, New York.

One week later, about 70 per cent of those who took psilocybin still had a symptom score reduction of about 35 per cent, and the benefits persisted at a 12-week follow-up. “It’s definitely better and faster than other medications for OCD,” says Nutt, who was involved in a recent trial, without a placebo group, that similarly found a small dose of psilocybin reduced OCD symptoms.

Kwan says that seeing such an improvement in people who have already tried multiple standard treatments suggests that psilocybin engages the brain in a fundamentally different way, but it’s exact mechanism for helping with OCD is unclear. “If we can uncover that biology, it could transform how we think about treating many psychiatric disorders, not only OCD,” he says.

One idea is that psilocybin enhances the plasticity of the brain, so thoughts that seemed rigid and dominant can become less powerful. That is profound with all psychedelics, says Nutt, who recently showed that one dose of the psychedelic DMT reduces symptoms of depression. “Previously, depressive thoughts dominated their thinking, but after the psychedelic, their brain is more flexible and they put them to one side instead of agreeing with them,” he says.

Another idea is that psilocybin recalibrates the relationship between the brain’s default mode network, which is involved in rumination and the sense of self, and its other areas, says Pittenger. We also know that a single dose of psilocybin can rewire the brain and it may also boost mental health by dampening inflammation.

But the drug could have safety concerns. During the Yale study, one person who already had persistent thoughts about suicide began to actively plan it. The danger passed after standard monitoring, but Pittenger says this highlights the need for clinical safeguards to be in place when administering psilocybin medically. Larger trials also need to confirm the drug’s efficacy and safety, the best dose and who is most likely to benefit or be at risk, he says.

Another recurring problem with psychedelic research is that the effects of these drugs mean that trial participants can often gauge whether they have received them or a placebo. The team tried to mitigate this by using a dose of niacin that can cause psychedelic-like events, such as flushing and an elevated heart rate. Nevertheless, Pittenger says that most of the participants still had a clear idea of what they had received. “This is a weakness of the study, as it is for most such work in the field,” he says.

Need a listening ear? UK Samaritans: 116123; US 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline: 988; hotlines in other countries.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant medical breakthrough where a single dose of psilocybin provides rapid and lasting relief for OCD symptoms in patients unresponsive to conventional treatments. The study is a placebo-controlled trial with meaningful clinical impact, offering hope for millions affected by OCD worldwide. It contains sufficient context about the trial, results, and potential mechanisms, highlighting a promising new treatment avenue with broad societal benefits.
articles.personality_title: Single dose of magic mushroom ingredient eases OCD symptoms for months
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) is a mental health condition where people have repeated, unwanted thoughts and behaviors. Many people with OCD don’t get better with usual treatments like therapy or medicine.

**What happened** – Researchers at Yale tested a medicine called psilocybin, found in magic mushrooms, on 28 adults with OCD. Half got psilocybin, and half got a placebo (a fake pill). Those who took psilocybin had much lower OCD symptoms just two days later. The improvement lasted for at least 12 weeks after one dose.

**Impact** – This is the first study using a placebo to prove psilocybin can quickly reduce OCD symptoms when other treatments fail. It suggests psilocybin works differently in the brain, helping people break out of obsessive thoughts and habits. This could change how OCD and other mental health problems are treated.

**What’s next step** – Scientists need bigger studies to check how safe psilocybin is, find the best dose, and learn who benefits most. They also want to understand exactly how it changes the brain to help with OCD.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A single dose of psilocybin from magic mushrooms can reduce OCD symptoms for months in people who didn’t improve with other treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une dose unique de l’ingrédient des champignons magiques soulage les symptômes du TOC pendant des mois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le trouble obsessionnel compulsif (TOC) est une maladie mentale où les personnes ont des pensées et des comportements répétitifs et non désirés. Beaucoup de personnes atteintes ne s’améliorent pas avec les traitements habituels comme la thérapie ou les médicaments.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de Yale ont testé un médicament appelé psilocybine, présent dans les champignons magiques, sur 28 adultes atteints de TOC. La moitié a reçu de la psilocybine, l’autre moitié un placebo (un faux médicament). Ceux qui ont pris de la psilocybine ont vu leurs symptômes baisser beaucoup en seulement deux jours. Cette amélioration a duré au moins 12 semaines après une dose unique.

**Impact** – C’est la première étude avec placebo qui montre que la psilocybine peut rapidement réduire les symptômes du TOC quand d’autres traitements échouent. Cela suggère que la psilocybine agit différemment dans le cerveau, aidant les personnes à sortir de leurs pensées et habitudes obsessionnelles. Cela pourrait changer la façon dont on traite le TOC et d’autres problèmes de santé mentale.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques doivent faire des études plus grandes pour vérifier la sécurité de la psilocybine, trouver la meilleure dose et comprendre qui en bénéficie le plus. Ils veulent aussi savoir comment elle agit dans le cerveau pour aider avec le TOC.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une seule dose de psilocybine provenant des champignons magiques peut réduire les symptômes du TOC pendant des mois chez des personnes qui n’ont pas été améliorées par d’autres traitements.
articles.personality_title_es: Una sola dosis del ingrediente de los hongos mágicos alivia los síntomas del TOC durante meses
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El trastorno obsesivo-compulsivo (TOC) es una enfermedad mental donde las personas tienen pensamientos y comportamientos repetitivos no deseados. Muchas personas con TOC no mejoran con los tratamientos habituales como la terapia o los medicamentos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Yale probaron un medicamento llamado psilocibina, que se encuentra en los hongos mágicos, en 28 adultos con TOC. La mitad recibió psilocibina y la otra mitad un placebo (una pastilla falsa). Los que tomaron psilocibina tuvieron una gran reducción de sus síntomas en solo dos días. La mejora duró al menos 12 semanas después de una sola dosis.

**Impacto** – Este es el primer estudio con placebo que demuestra que la psilocibina puede reducir rápidamente los síntomas del TOC cuando otros tratamientos no funcionan. Esto sugiere que la psilocibina actúa de forma diferente en el cerebro y ayuda a romper los pensamientos y hábitos obsesivos. Esto podría cambiar cómo se trata el TOC y otros problemas de salud mental.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos necesitan estudios más grandes para verificar la seguridad de la psilocibina, encontrar la dosis ideal y entender quién se beneficia más. También quieren saber cómo cambia el cerebro para ayudar con el TOC.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una sola dosis de psilocibina de los hongos mágicos puede reducir los síntomas del TOC durante meses en personas que no mejoraron con otros tratamientos.
articles.image_prompt: A serene, softly glowing brain-shaped garden with winding paths breaking free from rigid, thorny vines into open, flowing streams of light and color, symbolizing the easing of obsessive thoughts and lasting mental clarity after a single dose of psilocybin.
